Ah, okay, that makes perfect sense then. From <https://docs.python.org/2/library/ctypes.html#ctypes.byref>:

> Returns a light-weight pointer to obj, which must be an instance of a ctypes type.

Meaning that in Python, `ctypes.byref(thing)` is (roughly) equivalent to `&thing` in C. It's used here in places like:

```py
... _ssl.d2i_ECPrivateKey(ctypes.byref(self.k), ...)
```

Because `self.k` represents a `EC_KEY*` and the C [signature for d2i_ECPrivateKey](https://www.openssl.org/docs/manmaster/crypto/d2i_ECPrivate_key.html) is:

```c
... d2i_ECPrivateKey(EC_KEY **key, ...)
```

Hopefully that all makes sense.

In other words, now that you pointed out (and I bothered to read) the `ctypes` docs, the "cast" looks entirely appropriate. It was my mistake to think that it wasn't. I'll clean up the code (and my comment).

Well, you are quite right - `communicate()` CAN be trusted.

I did a small experiment of reading a Gbyte, both in PY27 and PY35, and they do not block: 

```python
>>> import subprocess as sb
>>> proc=sb.Popen('dd if=/dev/zero bs=1024 count=1000000', bufsize=0, stdin=sb.PIPE, stdout=sb.PIPE, stderr=sb.PIPE)
>>> a,b=proc.communicate()
```

I don't remember when I got this mistrust for `communicate()`, maybe when I had tried PY33, or maybe when epxerimenting with interactive processes (like the `git.cmd.Git.cat-file` persistent command); there the `comminicate()` is not suitable because it will start consuming streams till the death of the process, but the process is awaiting for more input from python-side before ending, so it's a deadlock.

One or two changes in my *windows* fixes were under this wrong assumption that `communicate()` should not be trusted.  But mostly I replaced code accessing directly `proc.stderr/stdout` from the main thread - this is definitely freeze-prone code.

Annotation file contains a dictionary with key-value pairs in each line where keys can be somewhat arbitrary strings and depend on the provider of the annotation file (see an example from ENSEMBL [here](https://www.ensembl.org/info/website/upload/gff.html)). The process maps the value of `ID attribute` input onto the dictionaries of the annotation file to obtain values.

If the labels in the `ID attribute` input of the process are different from the corresponding values, a user running the process from the front-end has no way of knowing which attribute is actually being selected prior to running the process. Furthermore, the user can be misled into thinking that the preferred attribute isn't available in the drop-down menu. I therefore believe that this change in the process is justified.

How about setting `seed` and `random` as class attributes instead?
```
class Model:
    seed =  int(dt.datetime.timestamp(dt.datetime.now())) % 39916801
    random =  numpy.random.RandomState(self.seed)
   
    def __init__(self, seed=None):
        if seed:
            self.seed=seed
            self.random=numpy.random.RandomState(self.seed)
```

This way anyone who subclasses `Model` can set their own instance attribute to override the class attribute. But if they don't want to override, there's still a good default they get without having to call  `super().__init__()` (and thing won't break if people forget to call `super().__init__()`

- message is a dictionary. Because then you get an extra set of quotes.
- message is a string with single quotes. They won't be escaped properly and, at least, loggly won't interpret it properly.

OK so here's a gotcha:

```File.name``` defaults to ```''```. This is saved in the Schema as a SimpleItem.

Ganga attempts to work out what the 'type' of ```''``` is but ```''``` could also be ```'myClass'```, due to the way that we allowed strings to be parsed (at least in the past).
As a result Ganga has to try and evaluate the object and work out if it is the latter or the former.
If the ```defvalue``` is the latter we do one thing and if the ```defvalue``` is ```''``` then we do another.

If we intend to keep supporting things the way they are atm we don't throw an exception. If we do then we break a lot of stuff, and frankly that was not the intention of this PR. I'm happy to remove the ```''``` special case but fixing every last bit of the schema here isn't what I wanted to do, nor what the PR set out to accomplish.
